violations.rule: Back brevity / atomic (no semicolon joining two facts)
violations.detail: Back contains two independent facts joined by a semicolon: 'Stack variables are tied to their scope and are popped when the function returns; heap variables persist until explicitly freed.' This should be split into separate cards or trimmed to answer only one side of the comparison.

violations.rule: Back brevity / atomic (no semicolon joining two facts)
violations.detail: Back is 'Stack: the compiler automatically; Heap: the programmer manually (or garbage collector)' — two distinct facts joined by semicolon. The self-check acknowledged this but excused it; SKILL.md explicitly lists semicolons as a violation pattern that 'MUST be split or trimmed.' Split into two cards.

Card 1 asks why stack is faster than heap (answer: contiguous/pointer bump). Card 3 asks why heap is slower than stack (answer: fragmentation/searching). These are the same comparison from two angles — knowing one largely gives you the other. Merge into one card or reframe one to test a non-obvious aspect.

violations.rule: Cloze difficulty check — guessable from context
violations.detail: 'Stack variables have {{c1::deterministic}} lifetimes because they are tied to their enclosing scope.' The word 'deterministic' is highly guessable given 'tied to their enclosing scope' — the surrounding context directly implies determinism. Restructure to remove the hint or test a different aspect.

Self-check claims ~55% why/how, but Card 2 ('What triggers a stack overflow?') and Card 4 ('What causes a memory leak?') are closer to definitional/factual than true 'why/how' reasoning cards. The actual why/how percentage is reasonable but the self-check is slightly inflated. Minor issue.
Back includes '(or garbage collector)' which introduces a new concept (garbage collection) as a parenthetical. This is a new fact, not an abbreviation. Should be its own card or removed.

score_justification: Good topic selection and no trivia/orphan/label-only problems. However, two cards violate the explicit semicolon/atomicity rule (cards 5 and 8), there is a mirror-deducible pair (cards 1 and 3), and a cloze is guessable from context (card 9). The self-check caught the semicolon issue in card 8 but incorrectly excused it.
